extra.message: User-controlled input params["id"] is concatenated directly into the SQL query string passed to cursor.execute() without any sanitization or parameterization. An attacker can inject arbitrary SQL, including UNION-based extraction and boolean/time-based blind injection.

User-controlled input params["comment"] is interpolated via % formatting directly into the SQL INSERT query string passed to cursor.execute(). This allows SQL injection to manipulate or extract data from the database.

The password parameter params.get("password", "") is concatenated directly into the SQL query string at /login. Although username is sanitized with re.sub(r"[^\w]", "", ...), the password field is not filtered at all, enabling SQL injection to bypass authentication (e.g., password=' OR '1' LIKE '1).

User-controlled input params["domain"] is concatenated directly into a shell command string passed to subprocess.run() with shell=True. An attacker can inject arbitrary OS commands by supplying a domain value like "example.com; rm -rf /" or using backticks/pipes.

pickle.loads() is called on user-supplied data from params["object"].encode(). Python pickle deserialization allows execution of arbitrary Python code. An attacker can craft a malicious pickle payload to achieve remote code execution.

User-controlled input params["path"] is passed to os.path.abspath() and then to open() without any restriction on allowed paths. An attacker can use path traversal sequences (e.g., ../../../etc/passwd) to read arbitrary files from the filesystem. Additionally, if the value contains '://', it is passed to urllib.request.urlopen(), enabling SSRF attacks.

When params["path"] contains '://', it is passed directly to urllib.request.urlopen() without any validation of the scheme, host, or URL. An attacker can make the server issue requests to internal services, cloud metadata endpoints (http://169.254.169.254/), or other unintended network resources.

User-controlled input params["include"] is used to read a local file or remote URL (via urllib.request.urlopen), and the resulting content is executed via exec(). This allows an attacker to execute arbitrary Python code by pointing to a file or URL containing malicious Python code.

User-controlled input params["name"] is interpolated via % formatting directly into an XPath expression string passed to lxml's .xpath() method. An attacker can inject XPath operators to manipulate query logic and extract arbitrary node data from the XML document, including passwords.

User-controlled input params["xml"] is parsed by lxml.etree.parse() with an XMLParser configured with load_dtd=True, resolve_entities=True, and no_network=False. This configuration enables XML External Entity (XXE) injection, allowing an attacker to read local files or perform SSRF through crafted XML DOCTYPE declarations with SYSTEM entity references.

Database query results (including user-controlled data stored via SQL injection) are interpolated directly into the HTML response without HTML-encoding. Values returned from cursor.fetchall() after the SQL injection-vulnerable query on line 30 are written to content and rendered in the browser without escaping, enabling stored or reflected XSS.

Comments retrieved from the database via cursor.fetchall() are interpolated directly into the HTML response without HTML-encoding. Since comments are stored from unsanitized user input (line 50), an attacker can store JavaScript in a comment and it will execute in all users' browsers when they view the comments page.

The HTML_PREFIX JavaScript block uses document.write() with decodeURIComponent(document.location.hash.substring(index + 5)) when the URL fragment contains 'lang='. The decoded hash value is written into the DOM without sanitization, enabling DOM-based XSS via a crafted URL fragment such as #lang=.

User-controlled input params["redir"] is inserted directly into a meta-refresh redirect tag (<meta http-equiv="refresh" content="0; url=..."/>) without validation. An attacker can redirect victims to arbitrary external URLs, facilitating phishing attacks.

User-controlled input params["size"] is cast to int and used to create a list of strings of length size^2 characters ("#" * int(params["size"]) for _ in range(int(params["size"]))). A large value such as size=100000 would attempt to allocate approximately 10GB of memory in a single request, causing a denial of service.

In the exception handler, traceback.format_exc() is returned as the HTTP response body when an exception occurs. Full Python stack traces reveal internal file paths, source code structure, and potentially sensitive configuration details to an attacker.

cwe: CWE-209: Information Exposure Through an Error Message

extra.message: Plaintext passwords for multiple user accounts ('7en8aiDoh!', '12345', 'gandalf', 'phest1945') are hardcoded directly in the USERS_XML constant, which is loaded into the in-memory database at startup. These credentials are exposed to anyone with access to the source code.

The /users.json endpoint wraps JSON output in a user-controlled callback function name from params["callback"] without any validation or sanitization. An attacker can inject arbitrary JavaScript as the callback name, enabling cross-site scripting via JSONP if the response is loaded in a script tag from a different origin.

User-controlled input params.get("charset", "utf8") is embedded directly into the Content-Type HTTP response header without validation. An attacker can inject CRLF characters (\r\n) via the charset parameter to inject arbitrary HTTP response headers or split the HTTP response.
The response explicitly sets 'X-XSS-Protection: 0', disabling the browser's built-in XSS auditor for all responses. This removes a browser-level defense against reflected XSS attacks.

When params["include"] contains '://', it is passed directly to urllib.request.urlopen() without any restriction on scheme or host. The fetched content is then executed as Python code via exec(). This enables SSRF to internal services and full remote code execution.
